title: 08 FourierSeries 4pp
body:
Aims MATH2019 вАУ Engineering Mathematics 2E Topic 8: Fourier Series I Many applications in engineering involve phenomena that have a cyclical or periodic behaviour. For example, the motion of a piston or the oscillation of an audio speaker. Dr Bill Ellis I You know that the sine and cosine functions are periodic. School of Mathematics and Statistics The University of New South Wales Fourier series provide us with a tool to analyse periodic Sydney, Australia phenomena by transforming periodic signals into a series Email: wjellis@unsw.edu.au (or infinite sum) of sines and cosines. I In this topic, you will learn how to represent a periodic function by its Fourier series. You will also learn how to solve the problem of forced oscillations (see Topic 5) using Fourier series. March 31, 2022 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

What you need to know What you will learn

I Calculate the Fourier series of a periodic function. I Extend a function given on the interval [0, L] (for some L > 0) to a periodic function defined on the whole real In this topic, periodic functions, as well as even and odd line. functions, are of great importance. You have learnt about such I Calculate the half-range Fourier expansion of a function functions in high school and/or first year. The first section given on the interval [0, L]. contains a summary of key facts. I Use Fourier series to solve a nonhomogeneous second order linear ODE when the right-hand side is a periodic function. I Apply this technique to solve the problem of forced oscillations.

. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Outline Periodic functions

Periodic Functions Definition 1 A function f is said to be periodic when there exists T > 0 Fourier Series such that if f is defined at x then it is also defined at x + T and Fourier Series with Arbitrary Period f (x + T ) = f (x). The smallest positive T satisfying the above identity is called Periodic Extensions the period of f . Note Half-Range Expansions The function f may be undefined at a number of points. If f is undefined at x0 , then f is also undefined at x0 ¬± T , Forced Oscillations and Fourier Series x0 ¬± 2T , вА¶.

Periodic functions Example 2 I sin x and cos x are periodic with period 2ѕА. I tan x is periodic with period ѕА, and is undefined at ¬±ѕА/2, ¬±3ѕА/2, вА¶.

Example 3 Show that the periods of sin(nx) and cos(nx) are T = 2ѕА/n. x вИТ 2T xвИТT x x+T Proof. Let f (x) = sin(nx). Then

f (x + T ) = sin[n(x + T )] = sin(nx + nT )

equals f (x) = sin nx for all x if and only nT = 2ѕА, because sin x has period 2ѕА. Similarly for cos(nx). Theorem 17 (Convergence of a Fourier series) Let f be a 2ѕА-periodic function. Assume that Example 16 I f is continuous on the interval[вИТѕА, ѕА], except possibly at
